1: Guard my life from the terror of the enemy — the "terror" (pahad) here is specifically psychological: the dread produced by sustained enemy threat. This is not a battlefield situation but a social one — the fear of what conspirators may do. Pahad appears in the prophets as the paralyzing dread that overtakes those who know enemies are planning against them (Isa 24:17-18; Jer 20:3-4). The psalmist asks for protection specifically from the emotional and psychological weight of the threat, not only from its physical consequences.
2: Shelter me from the conspiracy of the wicked — the key word is sod (secret counsel, conspiracy). This is the same word used for the divine council: the assembly of heavenly beings who deliberate with YHWH (Ps 89:7; Jer 23:18, 22; Job 15:8; Amos 3:7). The wicked have their own conspiratorial "council" that parodies the divine assembly — a secret gathering that plots against the innocent just as the heavenly council deliberates over the fate of nations. The psalmist implicitly appeals to the true sod (the divine council) to protect him from the false one.
3: They sharpen their tongues like swords and aim bitter words like arrows — the tongue-as-weapon metaphor is pervasive in the wisdom tradition (Prov 12:18: "reckless words pierce like a sword"; 25:18: "a false witness against a neighbor is a club, a sword, a sharp arrow"; Jer 9:8: "their tongue is a deadly arrow"). In the social world of the ancient court, verbal attacks — slander, false accusation, poisonous rumors — could be as lethal as physical assault. Legal and social ruin followed from well-placed false accusations, making the tongue genuinely dangerous.
4: Shooting from hiding places at the innocent; they strike suddenly and without fear — the method of cowardly attack: the ambush, the sudden strike, anonymity as cover. In ancient warfare, ambush was considered less honorable than open battle, because it did not give the opponent the chance to prepare or defend. The wicked's method reveals their character: they cannot defeat the innocent in open confrontation and so resort to stealth. "Without fear" — they act with impunity because they believe they are invisible (v5: "who will see us?").
5: Who will see us? — the assumption that secret plots escape divine notice is the practical atheism of the Psalter's wicked. Ps 94:7 articulates the same folly: "The LORD does not see; the God of Jacob does not perceive." The wicked function as practical atheists not by denying YHWH's existence but by behaving as though his perception is limited. This is the same error condemned in Ezek 8:12, where the elders of Israel say "The LORD does not see us; the LORD has forsaken the land" while conducting idolatrous worship in the dark.
6: The inward thought and heart of a person are unfathomable — a wisdom observation about the depth of human self-deception. Jer 17:9: "The heart is deceitful above all things and beyond cure — who can understand it?" The wicked's "perfect scheme" is self-assessed; they cannot see the depths of their own wickedness because the heart that generates the scheme is the same heart evaluating it. The irony is that they call their plan "complete" (tamam) — the same word used for blamelessness before YHWH — while it is in fact morally corrupt to its depths.
7: But God will shoot his arrow at them; suddenly they will be struck down — the divine reversal uses the same weapon the wicked used: arrows (vv 3-4). The lex talionis structure is not incidental — what the wicked aimed at the innocent comes back on them through divine action. This is the consistent moral grammar of the Psalter's justice: the pit the wicked dig for others they fall into themselves (Ps 7:15-16; 9:15-16; 35:8; 57:6). The divine arrow is "sudden" (pitom) — the same unexpected quality that characterized the wicked's own attack (v4).
8: God makes their own tongues their undoing — the precise reversal of v3: they sharpened their tongues like swords; their tongues become the instrument of their own destruction. The mechanism is not explained — the point is the structural justice. This reversal-of-weapon pattern appears in Esther (Haman hanged on the gallows he built for Mordecai, Esth 7:10), in Proverbs (Prov 11:8; 26:27: "the one who digs a pit falls into it"), and most dramatically in the cross — the instrument of execution becomes the instrument of resurrection.
9: Then all people will stand in fear; they will proclaim what God has done — the public nature of divine judgment is essential to its function in covenant theology. YHWH's vindication of the innocent is not a private transaction; it is meant to be seen, declared, and pondered by the entire community. This is the social function of the todah (thanksgiving) ceremony: the rescued person publicly declares what God did so that the community's faith is renewed (Ps 22:22-31; 66:16; 107:21-22). Divine justice that is not publicly visible cannot function as a moral framework for society.
10: The righteous will rejoice in the LORD and take refuge in him — the psalm closes with the community's response to witnessed divine justice. "Boasting" in the LORD (yithallal) is the technical term for covenant-confident praise: Jer 9:23-24 explicitly prohibits boasting in wisdom, strength, or wealth and directs all boasting to knowing YHWH. The "upright in heart" — those whose inward reality matches their outward profession — are the contrast to the wicked whose inward thought was corrupt (v6). The division of humanity that underlies Psalm 1 (the righteous and the wicked) is the lens through which Psalm 64 resolves.
